content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 93314

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5691 322-6341

June 9, 1976

Mr. James R. Christiansen
Office of the City Attorney
1216 State Street, Suite 802-04
Santa Barbara, California 93101

Dear Mr. Christiansen:

Thank you for your letter of June 1, 1976. I am enclosing,
as you requested, a copy of the Nina Code, which is an approved
model based upon the Code approved for the City of Santa Rosa.
In addition to the boiler plate provisions of this model Code,
the only other documentation that is required for the approval
of a Conflict of Interest Code of a city is a declaration of an
appropriate official to the satisfaction of the requirements of
Government Code §87311. Any reasonable notice of the proposed
adoption of the Conflict of Interest Code for the city would be
appropriate to satisfy this requirement.

As you correctly indicate, police officers possess perhaps
more discretion in carrying out public responsibilities than do
any other public officials. In addition, the scope of this discretion has been the subject of significant scholarly opinion.
(See Police Discretion - Kenneth Culp Davis, West Publishing
(1975).) Professor Davis, for one, argues that the attempt
should be made to channel and regularize the exercise of police
discretion. I agree wholeheartedly with these sentiments. However, in interpreting the Commission's regulations with respect
to "making or participating in the making of governmental decisions", 2 Cal.Adm.Code §1870(b) and (c), it is my view that a
code reviewing body may approve a Conflict of Interest Code for
a police department which does not designate all police officers.
The Commission has taken no formal position on this matter and
thus, I cannot speak with certainty regarding its future actions.
However, the foreseeability of normal "beat patrolmen" affecting
a private financial interest by virtue of his or her decision
making seems in many cases rather remote. Whenever, as you
indicate "commercial policy decisions" are normally made by
police officers, they should, in my estimation, be included as
designated officials within a Conflict of Interest Code. Even
absent the designation of police officers in a Conflict of
Interest Code, their actions are still covered by Government
Code §87100 et seq.

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only some very faint marks or artifacts visible at the top and in the upper right corner that are not clearly legible text.

[illegible]

James R. Christiansen
June 9, 1976 - Page Two


Thank you very much for your comments.

Sincerely,


Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
Chief, Conflicts of Interest
Division


/glb

Enclosure

The image appears to be almost entirely blank or white, with only a few very faint marks visible at the top and a couple of small dots scattered across the page. There is no clearly legible text content to transcribe from this document image.

embedding.qa_text: June 9, 1976 Mr. James R. Christiansen Office of the City Attorney 1216 State Street, Suite 802-04 Santa Barbara, California 93101 Dear Mr. Christiansen: Thank you for your letter of June 1, 1976. I am enclosing, as you requested, a copy of the Nina Code, which is an approved model based upon the Code approved for the City of Santa Rosa. In addition to the boiler plate provisions of this model Code, the only other documentation that is required for the approval of a Conflict of Interest Code of a city is a declaration of an appropriate official to the satisfaction of the requirements of Government Code §87311. Any reasonable notice of the proposed adoption of the Conflict of Interest Code for the city would be appropriate to satisfy this requirement. As you correctly indicate, police officers possess perhaps more discretion in carrying out public responsibilities than do any other public officials. In addition, the scope of this discretion has been the subject of significant scholarly opinion. (See Police Discretion - Kenneth Culp Davis, West Publishing (1975).) Professor Davis, for one, argues that the attempt should be made to channel and regularize the exercise of police discretion. I agree wholeheartedly with these sentiments. However, in interpreting the Commission's regulations with respect to "making or participating in the making of governmental decisions", 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(b) and (c), it is my view that a code reviewing body may approve a Conflict of Interest Code for a police department which does not designate all police officers. The Commission has taken no formal position on this matter and thus, I cannot speak with certainty regarding its future actions. However, the foreseeability of normal "beat patrolmen" affecting a private financial interest by virtue of his or her decision making seems in many cases rather remote. Whenever, as you indicate "commercial policy decisions" are normally made by police officers, they should, in my estimation, be included as designated officials within a Conflict of Interest Code. Even absent the designation of police officers in a Conflict of Interest Code, their actions are still covered by Government Code §87100 et seq. The image you provided is completely white, with no visible content or text. It appears to be either a blank page or an image that has been edited to remove all content. If you have another page or a different image, please provide it for better results. Thank you very much for your comments. Sincerely, Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. Chief, Conflicts of Interest Division /glb Enclosure The image you provided is mostly blank, with only a few small black dots visible in the upper right corner. There is no discernible text or content to transcribe or analyze further. If you have another page or another image, please provide it for better results.
